turns.content: I'm onboarding to a new embedded project and I keep seeing #[panic_handler] everywhere. It feels like the Rust standard library panic mechanism, but it's not quite the same since this is no_std land where there are no global destructors or main entry points.

That's a great observation. In std environments panics propagate through unwinding and eventually hit the standard library, but in no_std you need to define your own panic handler that takes an impl PanicHandler trait argument. The compiler generates a call site for this handler whenever a panic occurs so the program doesn't just stop abruptly on bare metal.
So if I want to log the panic message instead of crashing immediately, how do I wire that up? Do I need some specific crate like panic-probe or something?

You can use panic-probe which provides a simple way to register a callback that runs before the actual halt. But in many embedded systems people prefer defmt logging because it's lightweight and efficient. You'd define your own #[panic_handler] block where you call defmt::write!() with the message string, then let defmt handle the formatting without allocating.
What happens if I try to panic in a function that doesn't have any stack space available? Will the panic handler itself consume memory?

Good catch. If you're trying to log via heap allocation, you'll fail because there's no allocator in no_std environments until you bootstrap one. That's why defmt is so popular here—it formats messages without any dynamic memory allocations at all, making it safe for zero-allocation scenarios.

Ah, I see! So the panic handler becomes a way to handle fatal errors gracefully by logging them instead of just halting. That's like how my production system logs errors before shutting down, but here it's happening at compile time and runtime without needing extra heap.

Exactly. Think of the panic handler as your system's emergency log that captures critical failures before termination. It turns what could be a silent crash into a diagnosable event, which is crucial when you can't afford to lose data on bare metal.
Hey, I'm new to this job and the code is all no_std. I keep seeing panic handlers everywhere but my IDE crashes if I try to run it like a normal program. What's going on?

That's totally expected when you switch from std to no_std environments. In no_std, there is no runtime stack or standard library to catch exceptions the way your IDE does in desktop apps. Instead of catching panics automatically, the panic handler becomes the actual entry point for error handling on embedded systems.
So if I call println and it panics, my program just dies? That seems risky compared to how normal Rust programs work with try blocks or result types.

Exactly, which is why you need a panic handler. Without one, the process halts immediately on any panic. In no_std contexts like microcontrollers, you use #[panic_handler] to define custom behavior instead of letting it crash. You can log messages via defmt or use crates like panic-probe to handle errors gracefully.
But wait, shouldn't we just avoid panics entirely? I thought Rust was supposed to be safe from crashes?

You're right that Rust aims for safety through ownership and lifetimes. But in no_std, you still need explicit panic handlers because there's no global exception mechanism like std::panic::catch_unwind. The panic handler lets you choose whether to log via defmt or just halt the system entirely depending on your safety requirements.
So if I want to avoid panics completely, I should use try blocks everywhere? But what about when something really bad happens like a hardware failure?

For normal logic errors, yes, you can use try blocks and let the compiler handle it. But for hardware failures or unrecoverable bugs, panics are sometimes intentional because they trigger immediate shutdowns that might be safer than continuing with corrupted state. The panic handler determines whether those crashes become fatal or get logged instead.
I see. So defmt is the logging mechanism here? Is it faster than println?

Yes, defmt is designed specifically for embedded systems where you want zero-cost logging without allocating memory. Unlike println which requires heap allocation and parsing, defmt messages are parsed directly by the hardware at compile time using traits like defmt::Format.
That sounds efficient but what if I need to log more complex data structures? Can I still use them with defmt?

You can define custom traits for complex types so they implement the required trait interface. For example, you might create a struct that implements defmt::Format and then log it alongside simpler values like usize or String without worrying about allocation overhead.
Wait, but what if I don't want to panic at all? Can I disable panics entirely?

You can set the panic handler to halt without logging or use no-alloc panic messages that avoid dynamic allocation. But you still need some form of panic handling in no_std because Rust won't catch panics automatically like it does with std libraries.
Okay, so the key takeaway is that #[panic_handler] gives us control over what happens when things go wrong. Thanks for walking me through this!
You're welcome! Just remember to keep your panic handlers minimal and use defmt whenever possible since it avoids the overhead of println in constrained environments.

I thought since I'm using a panic handler, panics would just print to stderr like in std and then the program would keep running. That way I could catch errors gracefully without crashing my microcontroller.

That's a common misconception when coming from standard Rust environments. In no_std contexts with embedded hardware, panics are far more dangerous because they don't have a default handler to return an error code or print logs safely. Without #[panic_handler], a panic will immediately halt the processor forever since there is no stack unwinding or cleanup phase available in bare-metal setups.

Oh right, so my microcontroller just stops dead? That's frustrating because I was trying to implement a graceful shutdown logic instead of letting it crash. Can't I just catch panics and log them instead?

You can't really 'catch' panics in the same way you do with try blocks or Result types since panic is not an exception but a runtime abort mechanism by design. Instead, you must install #[panic_handler] before your main function runs so that when a panic occurs, it logs the message via defmt or another logging channel and then resets the device to a safe state rather than hanging indefinitely.
Okay, so I need to use panic-probe crate? Is that the only way to do this without allocating memory since my heap is tiny?

The panic-probe crate allows you to recover from a panic by catching it at runtime and executing custom code, but using it does involve some allocation which might be problematic on very constrained systems. For truly no-alloc panics, the standard approach involves implementing #[panic_handler] directly in your crate root or using crates like defmt that provide minimal logging without heap usage.
I see, so if I want to avoid any panic handling overhead, I should just disable panics entirely? That sounds safer than trying to recover from them.

Yes, disabling panics via const fn is often the cleanest approach for safety-critical embedded code where you cannot afford any runtime overhead or unexpected behavior. You can use no-panic macros or carefully structure your logic so that errors are returned as Result types instead of unwinding the stack, ensuring deterministic execution without relying on panic handlers at all.

I've been working a lot with web frameworks where errors just bubble up to the browser or return 500 responses, but when I look at embedded systems I'm wondering what happens if you panic there. It feels like my code should just crash and die immediately, right? Or does it get caught somewhere?

That's a great observation about how web frameworks handle errors versus how bare metal systems behave. In Rust on the web side, the panic unwinds the stack until an error handler is found or the process exits. However, in embedded environments using no_std where there is no standard library and often no operating system to catch exceptions, panics are much more dangerous because they can't be handled by a normal exception mechanism. Instead of bubbling up, a panic will halt execution forever unless you explicitly set up a #[panic_handler] that intercepts the signal before it stops the program.
So I'm imagining this is like my web server catching an error and returning HTML instead of crashing? But if I don't have a handler, does the CPU just stop executing instructions?

Exactly. Without a #[panic_handler], the panic unwinds the stack and terminates the process immediately without any cleanup or logging. This is why many embedded developers use crates like panic-probe to provide a fallback mechanism that logs the panic message before halting. You can configure this handler to run specific code, such as printing via defmt or writing to an external flash memory region, instead of just stopping instantly.
I see how useful that is for debugging during development, but I'm wondering if there's a way to make panics completely silent so the device keeps running? Like maybe catching them and doing nothing?

In theory you could write a #[panic_handler] that does no-op logging, but this is generally discouraged in production because it hides bugs. If your code panics silently, you won't know what went wrong until you can't reproduce the issue later. Most serious embedded projects prefer to log panics via defmt or another logging crate so you can catch them during testing and verify that your logic doesn't actually panic under real-world conditions.
That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how exactly the stack unwinds works differently here compared to my web apps. Does it just stop at a certain point?

When you have #[panic_handler] configured in no_std environments, the panic signal is intercepted before full stack unwind occurs. The handler receives control and can execute code like logging or resetting memory before terminating. This means your application doesn't necessarily crash immediately; instead it goes through a defined cleanup routine. In contrast, web frameworks often rely on higher-level error handling logic that prevents panics from reaching the runtime at all.
Okay so I'm thinking about how to implement this myself for my next project. Should I use panic-probe or is there something better? And what exactly does defmt do?

panic-prove is a great starting point because it provides a simple way to log panics before halting, and you can easily customize the logging behavior using defmt. Defmt is a zero-cost logging format that allows you to serialize panic messages without allocating memory on the heap, which is crucial in resource-constrained environments. This makes defmt particularly useful when combined with panic-probe because it ensures your logs are small and don't impact performance.

I'm starting to understand how valuable zero-cost logging is for my project, but I'm wondering if there's a downside to using defmt compared to standard println? Like maybe the code gets harder to read?

Defmt does require writing custom serialization logic for your types, which means you need to implement traits like Serialize for each struct you want to log. This adds some boilerplate code and requires careful type design, but it trades complexity for performance. Standard println uses the standard library's formatting machinery which allocates memory and strings, whereas defmt avoids all of that by using a custom protocol designed specifically for embedded systems where heap allocation is undesirable.
I guess I'm still not sure how to actually set up the panic handler in my code. Does it go at the top of main or somewhere else?

You define #[panic_handler] before your main function, typically outside any other functions like init or setup. This ensures that if a panic occurs during initialization or runtime, it will be caught immediately rather than unwinding the entire stack. The handler can then execute code to log information via defmt and optionally reset memory regions before halting execution.

Alright so I think I get the big picture now: define the handler early, use defmt for logging without allocations, but I'm still curious about what happens if you panic during initialization. Does it even matter?

Panic during initialization is critical because it prevents your system from booting at all unless you have a recovery mechanism in place. Without a #[panic_handler], the device will never start running your application code, which means no logs or data collection can occur. This is why panic-probe and defmt are so valuable—they allow you to detect initialization failures early while still getting useful diagnostic information before the system completely stops.

Thanks for walking me through this! I think I finally understand how panics behave differently in no_std environments compared to my web apps, and how panic-probe helps catch them gracefully. One last question: can you show me a simplified example of setting up defmt logging?

Sure, here's a basic setup where you define your panic handler early using #[panic_handler] and configure it to log via defmt before halting. You'll need to enable the defmt feature in your Cargo.toml and implement traits for your types so they can be serialized without heap allocation.
